query_multiple_columns.label: Writing Clear Multi-Column Queries
query_multiple_columns.summary: Format and order several selected columns so a result is easy to understand.

sketch1.title: Format the Query for Easy Review

sc_columns_comma.title: How to list multiple columns
sc_columns_comma.prompt: Which query correctly selects the `name` and `price` columns from the `products` table?
sc_columns_comma.hint: Look for the query that separates the two column names with a comma.

help.concept: In SQL, multiple selected columns are written in the `SELECT` clause as a comma-separated list before the `FROM` clause.
help.hint_1: Check whether `name` and `price` are both in the `SELECT` part and whether `products` appears after `FROM`.
help.hint_2: A correct pattern looks like `SELECT first_column, second_column FROM table_name`.

sc_result_order.title: Column order in results
sc_result_order.prompt: If you run `SELECT category, name FROM products;`, which column appears first in the results table?
sc_result_order.hint: The results table follows the same order as the columns listed after `SELECT`.

help.concept: The order of columns in a `SELECT` statement controls the left-to-right order of columns in the query result.
help.hint_1: Read the query from left to right after the word `SELECT`.
help.hint_2: The first listed column becomes the first column shown in the results table.

mc_valid_multiple_column_queries.title: Find the valid queries
mc_valid_multiple_column_queries.prompt: Which queries correctly select more than one column from `products`? Choose all that apply.
mc_valid_multiple_column_queries.hint: Valid queries need `SELECT`, a comma-separated column list, and `FROM products`.

help.concept: A valid multi-column `SELECT` query needs correctly named columns, commas between them, and the table name after `FROM`.
help.hint_1: Check each option for missing commas or a misplaced `FROM` clause.
help.hint_2: More than one option uses the right structure: `SELECT column1, column2 FROM products;`.

mc_columns_in_products.title: Columns you can select
mc_columns_in_products.prompt: Which of these are real column names in the `products` table? Choose all that apply.
mc_columns_in_products.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove choices from unrelated topics.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that directly matches the question without relying on answer wording.

dr_query_structure_two_columns.title: Build a two-column query
dr_query_structure_two_columns.prompt: Arrange the tokens to form a correct query that selects `name` and `category` from `products`.
dr_query_structure_two_columns.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

dr_query_structure_three_columns.title: Order a three-column query
dr_query_structure_three_columns.prompt: Arrange the tokens to form a correct query that selects `id`, `name`, and `price` from `products`.
dr_query_structure_three_columns.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Think about which part must come first.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that directly matches the question without relying on answer wording.

fb_missing_column_name.title: Complete the SELECT list
fb_missing_column_name.prompt: Choose the missing column name to complete the query.
fb_missing_column_name.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

fb_missing_table_name.title: Complete the FROM clause
fb_missing_table_name.prompt: Choose the missing table name to complete the query.
fb_missing_table_name.hint: This lesson uses one table named `the missing term`.

help.concept: The `FROM` clause tells SQL which table contains the columns named in the `SELECT` clause.
help.hint_1: The columns `id` and `name` both come from the same table used throughout this topic.
help.hint_2: Use the table name shown in the schema for this lesson, not a column name.

ci_select_name_category.title: Show names and categories
ci_select_name_category.prompt: Write a query that returns the `name` and `category` columns from the `products` table. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check the results table.
ci_select_name_category.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson.

help.concept: This SQL exercise checks whether your query returns the requested result.
help.hint_1: Check the table name and selected columns in the SQL editor.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the results table.

ci_select_name_category.starterCode:
-- Return the name and category columns from products
-- add columns here
SELECT
FROM products;

ci_select_three_columns.prompt: Write a query that returns the `id`, `name`, and `price` columns from the `products` table, in that order. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check the results table.

help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the results table.

ci_select_category_stock_created.title: Choose a different set of columns
ci_select_category_stock_created.prompt: Write a query that returns the `category`, `stock`, and `created_at` columns from the `products` table. Keep the columns in that exact order. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check the results table.
ci_select_category_stock_created.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson.

try-query_multiple_columns-sketch0.title: Control the Result Column Order
try-query_multiple_columns-sketch0.prompt: A price-first export should place amounts before product labels. Return `price` first and `name` second from `products`.
try-query_multiple_columns-sketch0.hint: The left-to-right order after SELECT becomes the result-column order.

help.concept: A useful column sequence follows the way the reader will interpret the result.
help.hint_1: The left-to-right order after SELECT becomes the result-column order.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the returned columns, rows, and order with the expected result.

try-query_multiple_columns-sketch1.title: Query id and name from products
try-query_multiple_columns-sketch1.prompt: Build a product-catalog query that returns `id`, `name`, `price` from the `products` table. Run it and compare the result with the expected table.
try-query_multiple_columns-sketch1.hint: Use the query pattern from the lesson, then adjust the selected columns, table, or filter for this scenario.

help.concept: This SQL exercise checks whether your query returns the requested result.
help.hint_1: Check the selected columns and table name in the SQL editor.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the result table with the expected result.

try-query_multiple_columns-sketch1.starterCode:
-- Return id, name, and price from products

FROM products;

try-query_multiple_columns-sketch2.title: Format a Three-Column Inventory View
try-query_multiple_columns-sketch2.prompt: Prepare a readable inventory extract. Select `category`, `stock`, and `created_at` from `products`, in that order, with each selected column on its own line.
try-query_multiple_columns-sketch2.hint: Separate the three selected columns with commas and keep FROM products after the list.

help.concept: Readable formatting makes a longer SELECT list easier to scan without changing the result.
help.hint_1: Separate the three selected columns with commas and keep FROM products after the list.
help.hint_2: Run the query and compare the returned columns, rows, and order with the expected result.

column_sequence.title: Build a Useful Column Sequence
column_sequence.bodyMarkdown:
A multi-column query should present information in a useful sequence. Put identifiers and labels first, then the details that describe them.

```sql
SELECT
    id,
    name,
    category,
    stock
FROM products;
```

This result is easy to scan: the row begins with the product identity, then shows its category and inventory count. SQL does not decide this order for you—the order of the selected columns becomes the order of the result.

When planning a report, imagine reading one row from left to right. Arrange the columns so the row tells a clear story.

readable_formatting.title: Format the Query for Easy Review
readable_formatting.bodyMarkdown:
SQL ignores most whitespace, so formatting is for people. Put each selected column on its own line when the list grows.

```sql
SELECT
    name,
    price,
    created_at
FROM products;
```

The commas separate columns; the final selected column has no trailing comma. `FROM` begins on a new line so the source is easy to find.

Readable formatting makes missing commas, misspelled columns, and incorrect clause order easier to spot. A query should be understandable before it is run.
